guest_role: CEO of PagerDuty (public company); board member at Estee Lauder Companies
assertiveness_why: Harry asks personal, reflective, well-prepared follow-up questions and occasionally injects his own struggles for comparison, but he never challenges or contests Jennifer's claims.
question_style: Mostly open-ended and reflective, often turning the guest's answer into a personal ask for advice about himself (e.g. risk-taking, impatience); he admits abandoning the prepared schedule after the first question and does not interrupt or push back.
tone: Warm, admiring, and personal, verging on a mentorship/advice-seeking dynamic rather than a journalistic interview; self-deprecating about his own impatience and self-doubt.
depth: Goes past surface leadership platitudes into specific mechanics: board composition and curation, decision-making with imperfect information, building a culture of internal debate (Lencioni's Five Dysfunctions), and concrete diversity-as-business-initiative tactics.
harry_quotes: How did you get comfortable making fast decisions with imperfect information?
What can specifically be done? Because I'm so with you.
Do you have a process around which you just exclude yourself from the world?
learnings: Serving on boards trains executives to make confident calls with only a fraction of the data they'd have as an operator, building comfort with decisiveness.
A healthy, candid boardroom culture requires an explicit foundation of trust plus deliberate practice, especially for teams culturally prone to excessive politeness.
Good board members are distinguished less by how much they ask and more by the precision and timing of a few genuinely insightful questions.
Treating diversity and inclusion as a measurable business initiative with resourced goals and public reporting drives more change than mission-statement rhetoric.
Decisions made quickly under time pressure are often just as good, or better, than heavily analyzed ones, because speed enables faster course-correction.
notable: Harry admits he asked only his first scripted question and improvised the entire rest of the conversation.
